record.query: Retrieve only build number of current kernel, ie. #104

raw_output_payload.reasoning: We need bash command to get build number of current kernel. On Linux, uname -r gives version string like 5.15.0-104-generic. Build number is 104. So command: uname -r | awk -F- '{print $2}'? Actually second field after dash is 104. But sometimes there may be more dashes. For generic: 5.15.0-104-generic. So awk -F- '{print $2}'. That gives 104. So final command: uname -r | awk -F- '{print $2}'.

So final command: uname -r | awk -F- '{print $2}'.

task.query: Counts all business days in a current month.

record.query: Counts all business days in a current month.

command: first=$(date +%Y-%m-01); last=$(date -d "$first +1 month -1 day" +%Y-%m-%d); seq -f "%F" -s " " $(date -d "$first" +%s) $(date -d "$last" +%s) 86400 | awk '{cmd="date -d \""$0"\" +%u"; cmd | getline w; close(cmd); if (w<6) c++} END{print c}'

generation.generated_command: first=$(date +%Y-%m-01); last=$(date -d "$first +1 month -1 day" +%Y-%m-%d); seq -f "%F" -s " " $(date -d "$first" +%s) $(date -d "$last" +%s) 86400 | awk '{cmd="date -d \""$0"\" +%u"; cmd | getline w; close(cmd); if (w<6) c++} END{print c}'
generation.raw_output_text: first=$(date +%Y-%m-01); last=$(date -d "$first +1 month -1 day" +%Y-%m-%d); seq -f "%F" -s " " $(date -d "$first" +%s) $(date -d "$last" +%s) 86400 | awk '{cmd="date -d \""$0"\" +%u"; cmd | getline w; close(cmd); if (w<6) c++} END{print c}'

raw_output_payload.content: first=$(date +%Y-%m-01); last=$(date -d "$first +1 month -1 day" +%Y-%m-%d); seq -f "%F" -s " " $(date -d "$first" +%s) $(date -d "$last" +%s) 86400 | awk '{cmd="date -d \""$0"\" +%u"; cmd | getline w; close(cmd); if (w<6) c++} END{print c}'
raw_output_payload.reasoning:
We need bash command to count business days (Mon-Fri) in current month. Use date, seq, etc. Compute first day and last day. Then loop days, count if weekday not Sat Sun. Use GNU date. Command: 

```
first=$(date +%Y-%m-01); last=$(date -d "$(date +%Y-%m-01) +1 month -1 day" +%Y-%m-%d); seq -f "%F" -s " " $(date -d "$first" +%s) $(date -d "$last" +%s) 86400 | awk '{
  cmd="date -d \""$0"\" +%u";
  cmd | getline wday;
  close(cmd);
  if (wday<6) count++;
} END{print count}'
```

But need one line. Combine. Use bash one-liner. Let's craft:

```
first=$(date +%Y-%m-01); last=$(date -d "$first +1 month -1 day" +%Y-%m-%d); seq -f "%F" -s " " $(date -d "$first" +%s) $(date -d "$last" +%s) 86400 | awk '{w=system("date -d \""$0"\" +%u"); if (w<6) c++} END{print c}'
```

But system returns exit status, not output. Use getline. Use awk:

```
awk '{cmd="date -d \""$0"\" +%u"; cmd | getline w; close(cmd); if (w<6) c++} END{print c}'
```

So final command:

```
first=$(date +%Y-%m-01); last=$(date -d "$first +1 month -1 day" +%Y-%m-%d); seq -f "%F" -s " " $(date -d "$first" +%s) $(date -d "$last" +%s) 86400 | awk '{cmd="date -d \""$0"\" +%u"; cmd | getline w; close(cmd); if (w<6) c++} END{print c}'
```

Return that.
